text: Fever above 38°C (100.4°F) lasting more than 3 days requires medical evaluation. High fever above 40°C is a medical emergency. Paracetamol or ibuprofen can reduce fever. Keep hydrated. WHO recommends immediate care for fever with stiff neck, rash, or difficulty breathing.

Most headaches are tension-type or migraine and are not dangerous. Warning signs requiring urgent care: sudden severe headache (thunderclap), headache with fever and stiff neck (possible meningitis), headache after head injury, headache with vision changes or weakness.

Chest pain can indicate heart attack, especially if accompanied by pain radiating to arm or jaw, shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea. This is a medical emergency — call 112/911 immediately. Not all chest pain is cardiac; causes include acid reflux, muscle strain, or anxiety.

Abdominal pain causes range from mild (gas, indigestion) to serious (appendicitis, ulcer). Seek immediate care for: severe sudden pain, pain with fever, vomiting blood, or rigid abdomen. Appendicitis typically starts around navel and moves to lower right abdomen.

Difficulty breathing can indicate asthma, pneumonia, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, or COVID-19. Seek emergency care if breathing is severely labored, lips turn blue, or onset is sudden. Mild breathlessness with activity may indicate anemia or deconditioning.

Cough lasting more than 3 weeks is chronic and needs evaluation. Productive cough with yellow or green sputum may indicate bacterial infection. Blood in sputum requires urgent evaluation. Dry persistent cough can indicate allergies, asthma, or ACE inhibitor medication side effect.

Most sore throats are viral and resolve within 7-10 days without antibiotics. Strep throat causes severe pain, fever, swollen lymph nodes, no cough — requires antibiotic treatment. Seek care if difficulty swallowing or breathing, drooling, or muffled voice.

Dizziness can be vertigo, lightheadedness, or imbalance. Common causes: dehydration, low blood pressure, inner ear issues, anemia. Seek urgent care if dizziness occurs with chest pain, severe headache, double vision, difficulty speaking, or sudden weakness.

Nausea and vomiting are usually caused by viral gastroenteritis, food poisoning, or motion sickness. Stay hydrated. Seek care if vomiting persists more than 24 hours, contains blood, or is accompanied by severe abdominal pain, high fever, or signs of dehydration.

Skin rashes have many causes: allergic reaction, viral infection, contact dermatitis, or drug reaction. Seek urgent care for: rash with difficulty breathing (anaphylaxis), widespread blistering, purple non-blanching spots (possible meningococcal disease), or rash after tick bite.

Most back pain is mechanical and resolves within 4-6 weeks. Red flags: pain with weakness or numbness in legs, bladder or bowel dysfunction, pain after trauma, severe pain at rest or night, unexplained weight loss. Common causes: muscle strain, disc herniation, poor posture.

Persistent unexplained fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks should be evaluated. Common causes: anemia, thyroid disorders, diabetes, depression, sleep disorders, or infections like mononucleosis. Fatigue with weight loss, night sweats, or swollen lymph nodes requires prompt evaluation.

Symptoms of diabetes include excessive thirst, frequent urination, unexplained weight loss, blurred vision, slow healing wounds, and frequent infections. Fasting blood glucose above 126 mg/dL on two tests confirms diabetes. Type 1 can present acutely with diabetic ketoacidosis.

High blood pressure above 140/90 mmHg is usually asymptomatic. Hypertensive crisis above 180/120 can cause severe headache, vision changes, chest pain — this is an emergency. Lifestyle: reduce salt, exercise, limit alcohol, maintain healthy weight.

FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency. Stroke is a medical emergency — every minute brain cells die. Other symptoms: sudden confusion, vision loss, severe headache, loss of balance. Call 112/911 immediately.

Mild allergic reactions: hives, itching, runny nose. Anaphylaxis is severe and life-threatening: throat swelling, difficulty breathing, rapid pulse, dizziness. Use epinephrine auto-injector if available and call 112/911 immediately. Antihistamines treat mild reactions only.

Burning urination, frequent urge, cloudy or bloody urine may indicate urinary tract infection. More common in women. Upper UTI causes fever, back pain, nausea. Drink plenty of water. UTIs require antibiotic treatment — see a doctor.

Anxiety symptoms: excessive worry, restlessness, rapid heartbeat, sweating, difficulty concentrating. Panic attacks peak within 10 minutes and are not dangerous despite feeling severe. Treatment: cognitive behavioral therapy, relaxation techniques, and sometimes medication.

Depression symptoms: persistent sadness, loss of interest, sleep changes, appetite changes, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, feelings of worthlessness. Seek immediate help for suicidal thoughts. Depression is treatable with therapy and medication. Exercise and social connection help.

Signs of wound infection: increasing pain, redness spreading beyond wound edges, warmth, swelling, pus or discharge, fever. Red streaking from wound indicates spreading infection requiring immediate care. Clean wounds with clean water, apply antiseptic, keep covered.

Red eye causes: conjunctivitis (pink eye), allergies, dry eye, foreign body, glaucoma. Seek urgent care for: sudden vision loss, severe eye pain, halos around lights, eye injury, or chemical exposure. Bacterial conjunctivitis needs antibiotic eye drops.

Ear pain is commonly caused by ear infection (otitis media or externa), jaw problems, or referred pain from throat. Children are more prone to middle ear infections. Symptoms: pain, discharge, hearing loss, fever. Severe or persistent ear pain needs medical evaluation.

Toothache causes: dental decay, abscess, cracked tooth, gum disease. A dental abscess (swelling, fever, severe pain) requires urgent dental care — infection can spread. Over-the-counter pain relief and clove oil can provide temporary relief but do not replace dental treatment.

Joint pain causes: osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, injury, or infection. Gout causes sudden severe pain usually in big toe. Septic arthritis (infected joint) is an emergency — hot, swollen joint with fever. Rheumatoid arthritis affects multiple joints symmetrically.

Muscle pain commonly caused by overuse, tension, or minor injury. Muscle cramps are sudden involuntary contractions — often from dehydration or electrolyte imbalance. Severe or persistent muscle pain with weakness, swelling, or dark urine (myoglobinuria) requires urgent evaluation.

Numbness or tingling can result from nerve compression, poor circulation, vitamin B12 deficiency, diabetes, or neurological conditions. Sudden onset numbness on one side of the body with weakness or speech difficulty is a stroke emergency — call 112/911 immediately.

Heart palpitations are feelings of a racing, fluttering, or pounding heart. Common causes: anxiety, caffeine, dehydration, thyroid issues, or arrhythmia. Seek urgent care if palpitations occur with chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting, or if they are prolonged.

Fainting (syncope) is brief loss of consciousness due to reduced blood flow to brain. Common causes: standing too quickly, dehydration, heat, or vasovagal response. Lay person flat and elevate legs. Seek care if fainting is recurrent, occurs with chest pain, or after exertion.

Swollen lymph nodes are usually a sign of infection. Common locations: neck, armpits, groin. Typically resolve within 2-4 weeks. Seek evaluation if nodes are very large, hard, painless, persist more than 4 weeks, or are accompanied by fever, night sweats, or unexplained weight loss.

Unintentional weight loss of more than 5% body weight over 6-12 months requires medical evaluation. Causes: hyperthyroidism, diabetes, cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, depression, or malabsorption. Always investigate unexplained weight loss in adults over 50.

Insomnia is difficulty falling or staying asleep affecting daytime function. Causes: stress, anxiety, depression, poor sleep habits, medications, or sleep apnea. Sleep hygiene: consistent schedule, dark cool room, avoid screens before bed, limit caffeine after noon.

Constipation is fewer than 3 bowel movements per week. Common causes: low fiber diet, dehydration, inactivity, medications. Increase water and fiber intake, exercise regularly. Seek care if constipation is new, severe, or accompanied by blood in stool, weight loss, or abdominal pain.

Diarrhea is loose watery stools more than 3 times daily. Usually caused by infection, food intolerance, or medication. Most resolve within 2-3 days. Key risk: dehydration — drink oral rehydration solution. Seek care if diarrhea contains blood, lasts more than 3 days, or with high fever.

Heartburn is burning sensation in chest caused by stomach acid rising into esophagus. Triggers: spicy food, fatty food, lying down after eating, alcohol, caffeine, obesity. Lifestyle: eat smaller meals, avoid triggers, don't lie down for 3 hours after eating, elevate bed head.

UTI symptoms in women: burning urination, frequency, urgency, pelvic pain, cloudy or strong-smelling urine. Drink plenty of water. Cranberry juice may help prevent recurrence. Antibiotics are required for treatment. Recurrent UTIs (3+ per year) need further investigation.

Painful periods (dysmenorrhea) affect up to 90% of women. Primary dysmenorrhea: cramping from uterine contractions. Secondary: caused by endometriosis or fibroids. Treatment: NSAIDs (ibuprofen), heat, exercise. Severe pain disrupting daily life warrants gynecological evaluation.

Early pregnancy symptoms: missed period, nausea, breast tenderness, fatigue, frequent urination. Seek immediate care for: heavy vaginal bleeding, severe abdominal pain (possible ectopic pregnancy), severe vomiting causing dehydration. Take folic acid 400mcg daily before and during early pregnancy.

Hypothyroidism (underactive): fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, depression, dry skin, constipation. Hyperthyroidism (overactive): weight loss, rapid heartbeat, heat intolerance, anxiety, tremor. Both diagnosed by blood test (TSH level) and treated with medication.

Anemia symptoms: fatigue, weakness, pale skin, shortness of breath, dizziness, rapid heartbeat. Most common cause: iron deficiency from poor diet or blood loss. Other causes: vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, chronic disease. Diagnosed by blood count. Treatment depends on cause.

Kidney stones cause severe flank pain radiating to groin, nausea, vomiting, blood in urine. Pain comes in waves (renal colic). Drink large amounts of water. Most small stones pass spontaneously. Seek urgent care for fever with stone symptoms (indicates infection), severe pain, or inability to urinate.

Asthma causes episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and cough. Triggers: allergens, exercise, cold air, smoke, stress. Use reliever inhaler (salbutamol) for acute symptoms. Seek emergency care for severe attack not responding to reliever, blue lips, inability to speak in sentences.

Pneumonia symptoms: cough with phlegm, fever, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain when breathing. Can be bacterial, viral, or fungal. Risk groups: elderly, children under 5, immunocompromised. Bacterial pneumonia requires antibiotics. Severe cases need hospitalization with oxygen therapy.

Flu symptoms: sudden onset fever, muscle aches, headache, fatigue, dry cough, sore throat, runny nose. More severe than common cold. Rest, fluids, paracetamol for fever. Antiviral medication (oseltamivir) most effective if taken within 48 hours. Annual vaccination recommended.

COVID-19 symptoms: fever, dry cough, fatigue, loss of taste or smell, sore throat, headache, body aches. Most cases are mild. Seek emergency care for difficulty breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, or inability to stay awake. Isolate to prevent spread. Vaccination reduces severe illness.

Malaria symptoms: fever with chills and rigors in cycles, headache, muscle aches, fatigue, nausea, vomiting. Common in tropical regions. Can rapidly become severe with organ failure. Requires urgent blood test and antimalarial treatment. Use bed nets and repellents for prevention.

Dengue symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, pain behind eyes, muscle and joint pain, rash. Warning signs of severe dengue: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding gums, blood in urine, rapid breathing. No specific treatment — rest, fluids, paracetamol only (avoid aspirin/ibuprofen).

TB symptoms: persistent cough more than 3 weeks, coughing blood, chest pain, night sweats, fever, weight loss, fatigue. Spreads through air. Requires 6 month course of antibiotics. Drug-resistant TB is a growing concern. BCG vaccine provides partial protection in children.

High blood sugar symptoms: excessive thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision, headache, fatigue. In diabetics, caused by missed medication, illness, or too much carbohydrate intake. Very high blood sugar with vomiting and fruity breath smell is diabetic ketoacidosis — medical emergency.

Low blood sugar symptoms: shakiness, sweating, rapid heartbeat, dizziness, hunger, confusion, pale skin. Occurs mainly in diabetics taking insulin or oral medication. Immediate treatment: 15-20g fast-acting glucose (juice, glucose tablets, sugar). If unconscious, call 112 immediately.

Jaundice (yellow skin or eyes) indicates liver problem or bile duct obstruction. Causes: hepatitis, gallstones, liver disease, medication reaction. Other liver symptoms: abdominal pain (upper right), dark urine, pale stools, fatigue, nausea. Always requires medical evaluation.

Common STIs: chlamydia (often no symptoms), gonorrhea, syphilis, herpes, HPV, HIV. Symptoms may include genital discharge, sores, burning urination, pelvic pain. Many STIs have no symptoms — regular testing is important for sexually active people. All are treatable; HIV is manageable with medication.

Cellulitis is bacterial skin infection causing redness, warmth, swelling, pain, and sometimes fever. Commonly affects legs. Requires antibiotic treatment. Seek urgent care if red area is rapidly expanding, there are red streaks, blisters, fever above 38°C, or you feel very unwell.

Migraine: severe throbbing headache often on one side, lasting 4-72 hours, with nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. Aura may precede headache (visual disturbances, numbness). Triggers: stress, hormones, certain foods, lack of sleep. Treatment: triptans, NSAIDs, rest in dark room.

Seizure first aid: protect from injury, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth, time the seizure, place in recovery position after. Call 112 if: first seizure, lasts more than 5 minutes, no recovery, injury occurred, or patient has diabetes or is pregnant. Most seizures are self-limiting.

Early dementia symptoms: forgetting recent events, repeating questions, getting lost in familiar places, difficulty with everyday tasks, personality changes. Alzheimer's is the most common cause. Seek evaluation for any significant memory change. Some causes (B12 deficiency, thyroid, depression) are reversible.

Fever in children under 3 months needs immediate medical evaluation regardless of temperature. In older children, fever itself is not dangerous — it is the cause that matters. Warning signs: inconsolable crying, difficulty breathing, rash, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, extreme lethargy.

Dehydration signs in children: dry mouth, no tears when crying, sunken eyes, decreased urination, lethargy. Oral rehydration solution (ORS) is the treatment for mild to moderate dehydration. Continue breastfeeding. Seek urgent care if child is very lethargic, not drinking, has bloody diarrhea, or is under 6 months.

If poisoning suspected: call poison control immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless instructed. Bring the container to hospital. Common household poisons: cleaning products, medications, plants, pesticides. Medication overdose: call 112, do not leave person alone, note what and how much was taken.

For minor burns: cool with cold running water for 20 minutes, do not use ice, butter, or toothpaste. Cover loosely with clean non-fluffy material. Seek care for: burns larger than palm of hand, burns on face/hands/genitals/joints, chemical or electrical burns, burns with white or charred skin.

Signs of fracture: pain, swelling, bruising, deformity, inability to use limb. Immobilize the area, apply ice wrapped in cloth, elevate if possible. Do not try to straighten. Seek emergency care for open fractures (bone through skin), fractures with numbness, or if patient cannot bear weight.

Concussion symptoms: headache, confusion, dizziness, nausea, memory problems, sensitivity to light. Most concussions resolve with rest. Seek immediate emergency care for: loss of consciousness, repeated vomiting, seizure, one pupil larger than other, worsening headache, confusion, or slurred speech after head injury.

Alcohol withdrawal can be life-threatening. Symptoms begin 6-24 hours after last drink: tremors, sweating, anxiety, nausea. Severe withdrawal (24-72 hours): hallucinations, seizures, delirium tremens. Medical supervision essential for heavy drinkers stopping alcohol. Never stop suddenly without medical support.

Panic attack symptoms: sudden intense fear, racing heart, chest tightness, shortness of breath, trembling, sweating, dizziness, fear of dying. Peaks within 10 minutes and passes. Not physically dangerous despite feeling severe. Breathe slowly and deeply. Treatment: therapy, breathing techniques, sometimes medication.

Eczema causes itchy, inflamed, dry, cracked skin. Common triggers: soaps, detergents, stress, sweat, certain foods. Management: moisturize frequently, use mild soap, avoid triggers, topical corticosteroids for flares. Seek care if skin is infected (yellow crusting, oozing, hot, swollen).

Psoriasis causes red, scaly patches on skin, commonly on elbows, knees, and scalp. It is a chronic autoimmune condition, not infectious. Triggers: stress, infections, certain medications, alcohol. Treatments: topical creams, phototherapy, systemic medications for severe cases.

Acne is caused by blocked hair follicles with oil and dead skin cells. Types: whiteheads, blackheads, pimples, cysts. Management: gentle cleansing, non-comedogenic products, avoid squeezing. Treatments: benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, topical retinoids. Severe acne with scarring needs dermatologist evaluation.

Hair loss causes: male or female pattern baldness (genetic), alopecia areata (autoimmune), thyroid disease, iron deficiency, stress (telogen effluvium), medications. Sudden patchy hair loss or hair loss with other symptoms warrants medical evaluation. Most causes are treatable.

Nosebleed management: sit upright, lean slightly forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10-15 minutes. Do not tilt head back. Seek care if: bleeding does not stop after 20 minutes, caused by head injury, very heavy bleeding, taking blood thinners, or nosebleeds are recurrent.

Sinusitis symptoms: facial pain or pressure, nasal congestion, thick nasal discharge, reduced smell, headache, cough. Viral sinusitis (most common) resolves in 10 days without antibiotics. Use saline nasal rinse, steam inhalation. Seek care if symptoms worsen after 10 days or with high fever and severe facial pain.

Vertigo is sensation of spinning. Most common cause: benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) — brief episodes triggered by head movement. Meniere's disease causes prolonged vertigo with hearing loss and tinnitus. Epley maneuver can treat BPPV. Sudden severe vertigo with headache or neurological symptoms is an emergency.

Tinnitus is ringing, buzzing, or hissing in ears with no external sound. Causes: noise exposure, aging, ear infection, medication side effects, jaw problems. Usually not serious. Avoid loud noise, reduce caffeine. Sudden tinnitus with hearing loss or dizziness needs prompt evaluation.

Erectile dysfunction (ED) affects up to 50% of men over 40. Causes: cardiovascular disease, diabetes, hypertension, psychological factors, medications, low testosterone. ED can be an early warning sign of heart disease. Lifestyle changes help: exercise, healthy diet, quit smoking, reduce alcohol. Effective treatments exist.

Benign prostate enlargement (BPH) causes: weak urine stream, frequency, urgency, incomplete emptying, nocturia. Common in men over 50. Prostate cancer may have similar symptoms or none at all — screening with PSA test is debated. Seek care for blood in urine, pain urinating, or sudden inability to urinate.

Osteoporosis weakens bones, increasing fracture risk. Often no symptoms until fracture occurs. Risk factors: older age, female, low calcium intake, vitamin D deficiency, smoking, family history, steroid use. Prevention: calcium, vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, avoid smoking and excess alcohol.

Gout causes sudden severe joint pain, typically big toe, ankle, or knee. Joint becomes red, hot, swollen, extremely tender. Caused by uric acid crystal deposits. Triggers: alcohol (especially beer), red meat, seafood, sugary drinks, dehydration. Treatment: NSAIDs for attacks, allopurinol for prevention.

Appendicitis symptoms: pain starting around navel moving to lower right abdomen, fever, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite. Pain worsens with movement. This is a surgical emergency — do not eat or drink, seek emergency care immediately. Untreated appendicitis can rupture and cause life-threatening peritonitis.

Gallstone pain: severe pain in upper right abdomen, often after fatty meals, may radiate to right shoulder or back. Cholecystitis (inflamed gallbladder) adds fever and persistent pain. Seek urgent care for severe abdominal pain with fever or jaundice. Treatment is usually surgical removal of gallbladder.

Hernia is protrusion of organ or tissue through weakened muscle. Symptoms: bulge in abdomen or groin, pain when bending, coughing, or lifting. Most require surgical repair. Seek emergency care for: hernia that cannot be pushed back in, becomes very painful, red or dark, or if you have nausea and vomiting.

Varicose veins are enlarged twisted veins, usually in legs. Symptoms: aching, heaviness, swelling, itching. Risk factors: standing for long periods, obesity, pregnancy, family history. Lifestyle: elevate legs, compression stockings, regular walking. Seek care for sudden severe pain, redness, or skin ulcers.

DVT is blood clot in deep vein, usually leg. Symptoms: leg pain, swelling, warmth, redness. Risk factors: long immobility, surgery, cancer, oral contraceptives. Emergency if clot travels to lungs (pulmonary embolism): sudden chest pain, shortness of breath. Seek immediate care for any suspected DVT.

Anaphylaxis is life-threatening allergic reaction. Symptoms within minutes: throat swelling, difficulty breathing, rapid weak pulse, drop in blood pressure, pale or blue skin, loss of consciousness. Common triggers: bee stings, nuts, shellfish, medication. Epinephrine (EpiPen) is the only treatment — call 112 immediately.

Heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, cold pale clammy skin, weak pulse, nausea, fainting. Move to cool place, loosen clothing, apply cool wet cloths, sip water. Heat stroke is an emergency: hot red dry skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion, loss of consciousness — call 112, cool immediately.

Hypothermia (dangerously low body temperature): shivering, slurred speech, clumsy movements, confusion. Remove wet clothing, warm gradually with blankets, give warm beverages if conscious, seek emergency care. Frostbite: numbness, white or gray skin on ears, nose, fingers, toes. Do not rub — rewarm gently.

Food poisoning symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, sometimes fever, within hours of eating contaminated food. Usually resolves in 1-3 days. Focus on hydration. Seek care for: bloody diarrhea, high fever, symptoms lasting more than 3 days, signs of dehydration, or neurological symptoms.

If someone expresses suicidal thoughts: take it seriously, stay with them, listen without judgment, remove access to means, call emergency services or crisis helpline. Do not leave them alone. Suicidal crisis is a psychiatric emergency. In India: iCall 9152987821. International: find your local crisis line.

Contraception options: hormonal (pill, injection, implant, patch), barrier (condom, diaphragm), IUD (hormonal or copper), emergency contraception (within 72 hours). Condoms are the only method that protects against both pregnancy and STIs. Discuss options with a healthcare provider for best choice.

Common deficiencies: Vitamin D (fatigue, bone pain, muscle weakness — very common), Vitamin B12 (numbness, fatigue, memory problems — common in vegetarians), Iron (fatigue, pale skin — most common globally), Vitamin C (scurvy — rare). Diagnosed by blood test. Most correctable with supplements or dietary changes.

Dehydration symptoms: dark yellow urine, infrequent urination, dry mouth, dizziness, fatigue, headache. Severe: rapid heartbeat, rapid breathing, sunken eyes, confusion. Treatment: oral rehydration solution for mild to moderate. Severe dehydration needs IV fluids in hospital. Prevention: drink 8 glasses of water daily.

Blood in urine (visible or microscopic) always requires medical evaluation. Causes: UTI, kidney stones, bladder infection, kidney disease, or bladder cancer. Not always serious but should never be ignored. Other symptoms may be absent. Seek prompt medical care for any blood in urine.

Bright red blood in stool suggests lower GI source (hemorrhoids, anal fissure, colitis). Dark or tarry stool suggests upper GI bleeding (ulcer, esophageal varices) — this is more serious. Seek immediate care for: large amounts of blood, dark tarry stool, blood with abdominal pain, or unexplained weight loss.

Hemorrhoids cause rectal bleeding (bright red blood on toilet paper or in bowl), itching, discomfort. Very common — affecting 75% of people at some point. Treatment: high fiber diet, plenty of fluids, sitz baths, over-the-counter creams. Persistent or severe hemorrhoids may need medical procedures.

Acute bronchitis causes cough with phlegm, mild fever, chest discomfort. Usually viral — antibiotics not needed. Rest, fluids, honey for cough relief. Chronic bronchitis (cough with phlegm most days for 3+ months per year for 2+ years) needs medical evaluation and is usually linked to smoking.

Sleep apnea causes breathing to repeatedly stop during sleep. Symptoms: loud snoring, gasping during sleep, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness, difficulty concentrating. Risk factors: obesity, male sex, older age, smoking. Diagnosed with sleep study. Treatment: CPAP machine, weight loss, positional therapy.

Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep problems, memory issues, and mood disturbances. Predominantly affects women. No visible inflammation or tissue damage. Triggers: stress, poor sleep, illness. Treatment: exercise, sleep management, pain medications, cognitive behavioral therapy.

IBS symptoms: abdominal pain relieved by bowel movement, bloating, alternating constipation and diarrhea, mucus in stool. No structural abnormality. Triggers: certain foods (dairy, gluten, high-fat foods), stress, hormonal changes. Management: dietary changes, stress reduction, probiotics, fiber supplements.

Celiac disease is autoimmune reaction to gluten causing intestinal damage. Symptoms: diarrhea, bloating, abdominal pain, fatigue, weight loss, anemia. Many cases are atypical with few digestive symptoms. Diagnosed by blood test and biopsy. Treatment: strict lifelong gluten-free diet.

Lupus (SLE) is an autoimmune disease affecting many organs. Symptoms: butterfly-shaped facial rash, joint pain, fatigue, skin rashes worsened by sunlight, kidney problems, chest pain. Predominantly affects young women. Diagnosed by blood tests. Treatment: hydroxychloroquine, steroids, immunosuppressants.

MS symptoms: fatigue, vision problems, numbness or tingling, muscle weakness, balance problems, bladder issues. Course varies — relapsing-remitting is most common. Diagnosed by MRI and clinical criteria. Disease-modifying therapies can reduce relapses. Symptoms worsen with heat (Uhthoff phenomenon).

Parkinson's disease symptoms: tremor at rest, muscle rigidity, slowness of movement (bradykinesia), balance problems, small handwriting, soft speech. Symptoms start on one side. Non-motor symptoms: depression, sleep problems, constipation. Treatment: levodopa and other medications help manage symptoms.
